email_security.title: SPF (Sender Policy Framework)
email_security.description: A DNS-based email authentication protocol that specifies which mail servers are authorized to send email on behalf of a domain.

email_security.title: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)
email_security.description: An email authentication standard that uses public-key cryptography to verify that a message was sent and authorized by the owner of a domain.

email_security.title: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance)
email_security.description: An email authentication protocol that builds on SPF and DKIM to give domain owners control over how unauthenticated messages are handled and to enable abuse reporting.

email_security.title: ARC Authentication
email_security.description: Authenticated Received Chain preserves email authentication results across forwarding hops so receiving servers can evaluate the original authentication state.

email_security.title: Email Header Analysis
email_security.description: The process of examining RFC 5322 email headers to trace a message's delivery path, verify authentication results, and identify anomalies.

email_security.title: Homoglyph Domains
email_security.description: Domains that substitute visually identical or near-identical Unicode characters to impersonate legitimate domains.

email_security.title: Email Spoofing
email_security.description: The forgery of email header fields to make a message appear to originate from a sender other than its true source.

email_security.title: Link-Display Mismatch
email_security.description: A phishing technique where the visible anchor text shows a different URL than the actual href destination.

threat_intelligence.title: IOC Enrichment
threat_intelligence.description: The process of augmenting raw indicators of compromise with contextual threat intelligence.

threat_intelligence.title: Attack Surface Management
threat_intelligence.description: The continuous process of discovering, inventorying, and reducing an organization's externally exposed digital assets.

threat_intelligence.title: Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)
threat_intelligence.description: Observable artifacts that indicate a system or network may have been breached.

threat_intelligence.title: Threat Intelligence
threat_intelligence.description: Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging cyber threats.

threat_intelligence.title: Certificate Transparency
threat_intelligence.description: A public logging framework that records all SSL/TLS certificates issued by CAs.

threat_intelligence.title: Passive DNS
threat_intelligence.description: A historical record of DNS resolutions for domain-to-IP mappings.

threat_intelligence.title: WHOIS Lookup
threat_intelligence.description: A protocol for querying domain registration information.

threat_intelligence.title: Domain Reputation
threat_intelligence.description: A score or classification assigned to a domain based on historical behavior.

threat_intelligence.title: IP Reputation
threat_intelligence.description: A score or classification assigned to an IP based on malicious activity.

threat_intelligence.title: Threat Actor Profiling
threat_intelligence.description: The process of identifying and documenting threat group tactics and infrastructure.

threat_intelligence.title: DNS Security
threat_intelligence.description: Practices and technologies for protecting DNS infrastructure.

threat_intelligence.title: SSL/TLS Certificates
threat_intelligence.description: Digital certificates that authenticate server identity and enable encryption.

threat_intelligence.title: Open Ports
threat_intelligence.description: Network ports on a host that are accepting connections.

threat_intelligence.title: Vulnerability Scanning
threat_intelligence.description: The automated process of identifying security weaknesses.

forensics.title: Phishing Analysis
forensics.description: The forensic examination of suspected phishing emails.

forensics.title: Digital Forensics
forensics.description: The scientific discipline of identifying, preserving, and presenting digital evidence.

forensics.title: Incident Response
forensics.description: The organized approach to detecting, containing, and recovering from security incidents.

forensics.title: Timeline Analysis
forensics.description: The process of reconstructing a chronological sequence of events.

forensics.title: Log Analysis
forensics.description: The examination of system and network logs.

forensics.title: Malware Analysis
forensics.description: The process of examining malicious software behavior and capabilities.

detection_engineering.title: MITRE ATT&CK Framework
detection_engineering.description: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques based on real-world observations.

detection_engineering.title: Sigma Rules
detection_engineering.description: A vendor-agnostic open standard for writing detection rules.

detection_engineering.title: YARA Rules
detection_engineering.description: A pattern-matching tool for identifying and classifying malware.

detection_engineering.title: Threat Hunting
detection_engineering.description: The proactive search for adversary activity that has evaded security controls.

detection_engineering.title: Alert Triage
detection_engineering.description: The process of evaluating and prioritizing security alerts.

detection_engineering.title: SIEM
detection_engineering.description: Security Information and Event Management platform for log analysis.

detection_engineering.title: SOAR
detection_engineering.description: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response platforms.

detection_engineering.title: Detection-as-Code
detection_engineering.description: Managing detection rules as version-controlled code.

detection_engineering.title: API Security
detection_engineering.description: Practices and controls for protecting APIs.

attack_types.title: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
attack_types.description: A social engineering attack where adversaries hijack or spoof corporate email accounts.

attack_types.title: QR Phishing (Quishing)
attack_types.description: A phishing technique that embeds malicious URLs inside QR codes.

attack_types.title: Thread Hijacking
attack_types.description: An attack where an adversary compromises a mailbox and replies to existing threads.

attack_types.title: Spear Phishing
attack_types.description: A targeted phishing attack directed at specific individuals.

attack_types.title: Credential Harvesting
attack_types.description: The theft of usernames and passwords through fake login pages.

attack_types.title: Typosquatting
attack_types.description: Registering domain names that are deliberate misspellings of legitimate domains.

attack_types.title: OAuth Phishing
attack_types.description: An attack that tricks users into granting malicious OAuth consent.

attack_types.title: Social Engineering
attack_types.description: Psychological manipulation techniques to deceive people.

attack_types.title: Ransomware
attack_types.description: Malware that encrypts files and demands payment for restoration.

attack_types.title: Supply Chain Attack
attack_types.description: An attack that compromises a trusted third-party vendor.

attack_types.title: Watering Hole Attack
attack_types.description: An attack strategy where a website frequently visited by targets is compromised.

attack_types.title: Brute Force Attack
attack_types.description: A method that systematically tries large numbers of passwords.

attack_types.title: Insider Threat
attack_types.description: A security risk originating from within the organization.

spf.title: SPF (Sender Policy Framework)
spf.content:
## What is SPF?

Sender Policy Framework (SPF) is a DNS-based email authentication protocol that allows domain owners to specify which mail servers are authorized to send email on behalf of their domain.

## How It Works

1. Domain owner publishes a TXT record in DNS that lists the authorized sending IP addresses
2. Receiving mail server checks the SPF record before accepting the email
3. If the sending server is not listed, the email may be rejected or marked as spam

## SPF Record Syntax

v=spf1 ip4:192.0.2.0/24 include:_spf.example.com ~all

- v=spf1: Version identifier
- ip4: IPv4 address or network
- include: Includes another domain's SPF record
- ~all: Softfail (recommended)

## Best Practices

- Always use ~all (softfail) instead of -all (fail) initially
- Monitor reports before enforcing strict policy
- Keep DNS lookups under 10 to avoid validation failures

dkim.title: DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)
dkim.content:
## What is DKIM?

DKIM is an email authentication standard that uses public-key cryptography to verify that an email was not altered in transit and was sent by the claimed sender.

## How It Works

1. Sending server signs email headers with a private key
2. Public key is published in DNS as a TXT record
3. Receiving server verifies the signature using the public key

## DKIM Selectors

DKIM uses selectors to differentiate between multiple signing keys:
- default._domainkey.example.com
- google._domainkey.example.com
- selector1._domainkey.example.com

## Common Email Service Providers

- Google Workspace: google._domainkey
- Microsoft 365: selector1._domainkey
- Amazon SES: amazonses._domainkey
- SendGrid: s1._domainkey

dmarc.title: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance)
dmarc.content:
## What is DMARC?

DMARC builds on SPF and DKIM to give domain owners control over how unauthenticated messages are handled and enables aggregate reporting.

## DMARC Policy

- p=none: Monitor only, no action
- p=quarantine: Mark suspicious emails as spam
- p=reject: Reject suspicious emails

## DMARC Reports

- RUA: Aggregate reports (XML)
- RUF: Forensic reports (JSON)

## Example Record

v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com;ruf=mailto:forensic@example.com;fo=1
